exemplar.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Direct engagement with the prescribed title, maintaining focus on whether knowledge acquisition destroys wonder
Effective use of two Areas of Knowledge (the arts and the human sciences) throughout the essay
Inclusion of a variety of concrete, relevant examples (e.g., Preljocajâs Romeo and Juliet, the Mona Lisa, Netflix algorithms) that illustrate key points
weaknesses: Analysis of examples remains largely descriptive without sufficient depth or critical examination
Limited exploration and evaluation of counter-arguments and alternative points of view
Methodological considerations and explicit TOK terminology (ways of knowing, knowledge questions) are only briefly sketched or missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examiner awarded a total mark of 5, placing the essay at the lower end of band 5â6. The student demonstrates a clear focus on the title and employs relevant examples from two Areas of Knowledge, meriting recognition of coherent argumentation and illustrative support. However, pervasive descriptive treatment of examples, superficial engagement with counter-claims, and sparse methodological or TOK conceptual analysis prevent the work from reaching higher bands. In reverse grading to match the fixed total of 5, the single gradable criterion receives the full allocation of 5, reflecting the essayâs strengths in focus and illustration but also its clear limitations in depth and evaluation.

annotations.comment: The transition from arts to human sciences counter-argument is abrupt; a clearer signposting sentence would help maintain coherence and focus on the title.
The Netflix case study is topical but the integration of the source is clumsy; please use concise in-text citation and summarise key findings to improve flow.
Repeated URLs interrupt the essayâs narrative; consolidate external references in a works cited section and refer only to the author and date in the main text.

The studentâs restatement of the prescribed title accurately identifies scope but lacks nuance in defining key terms, leading to a broad thesis that is not yet tightly focused on a clear knowledge question.

The definitions of âwonderâ, âcuriosityâ and âamazementâ are descriptive but not critically linked to knowledge acquisition; the student should relate these definitions directly to ways of knowing and knowledge questions.
The concluding sentence of the introduction asserts a perspective without outlining the line of argument; the essay would benefit from an explicit roadmap indicating how each AOK will be analysed.

The discussion of the ballet example is vivid yet largely descriptive; the student should analyse how detailed knowledge of Shakespeareâs text deepens or diminishes wonder in choreographic interpretation.

Methodological reflection is missing: the student could discuss how artistsâ creative processes constitute a form of knowing that interacts with the sense of wonder rather than merely recounting the example.

The Shakespeare language example is interesting but the analysis stops at description; the student should explore how improved linguistic knowledge specifically alters layers of interpretation and wonder.
The choice of Elizabethan English terms to illustrate the interplay between knowledge and curiosity is well selected, demonstrating cultural and historical dimensions of the arts AOK.
The human sciences section opens broadly without specifying methodologies; the student should identify how psychological research methods inform their discussion of wonder.
The Maslow at Google example is promising but underdeveloped: clarify how the outcomes of this motivational model provoke or destroy wonder rather than simply describing workplace perks.
There is no explicit knowledge question interjection in this section; the student should embed TOK terminology to reflect on how knowledge about human motivation questions the nature of wonder.
The Carol Ann Duffy example is introduced but the analysis remains cursory; the student needs to explain how acquiring interpretive knowledge limits imaginative engagement with the poem.
This section would benefit from linking to conceptual frameworks around interpretation: discuss how authoritative explanations flatten alternative personal readings and reduce wonder.
The Mona Lisa analysis identifies historical research but does not critically examine the methodologies (scientific techniques) that demystify art; include reflection on the limits of explanation.
The use of the Mona Lisa as an example effectively illustrates how deep artâhistorical research can transform an artwork from source of wonder into an object of study.
The AI and algorithm example remains largely descriptive; deepen the analysis by explaining how explicit knowledge of data filtering processes reduces mystery and wonder.
There is no evaluation of ethical or epistemological implications; discuss whether predictable algorithmic recommendations undermine spontaneous wonder in media consumption.
Discussing confirmation bias would be stronger if framed within TOK vocabulary (e.g., ways of knowing) to show depth of critical thinking.
The academic reference to Atkinson and Kahn is correct but remains detached; the student should summarise the reviewâs conclusions to strengthen the argument.
Bringing in a contemporary Netflix study is a strong choice that connects human sciences to daily experiences, showing relevance of the essayâs question.
The doping example vividly illustrates confirmation bias but lacks direct reference to how that cognitive bias affects our sense of wonder about human achievement.
The synthesis beginning restates the dual role of knowledge but does not critically evaluate counterclaims; the conclusion would benefit from acknowledging nuance.
There is no discussion of varying degrees or contexts in which knowledge destroys wonder; consider qualifying statements to avoid overgeneralisation.
The final paragraph reiterates earlier points but fails to address broader implications or suggest further inquiry into the knowledge question.
A stronger conclusion would explicitly link back to ways of knowing and encourage reflection on how future knowledge acquisition might alter wonder differently.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
